items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.correct: They are random changes in DNA — caused by errors in replication or by mutagens — not directed by need.
items.misconception: Mutations occur because organisms need to adapt to their environment.
items.random_wrong: Mutations only happen in organisms that have been damaged by external chemicals.
items.why: Students confuse mutation with adaptation and think evolution involves organisms 'trying' to change — a Lamarckian rather than Darwinian view.

items.q: You compare bubbles/min from three DIFFERENT plant species. Which graph should you use?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bar chart. “Line graph” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Repeating an experiment three times mainly improves.
items.why: It's Reliability. “Validity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Three repeats at 20 cm give 18, 22 and 20 bubbles/min. What is the mean rate (bubbles/min)?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
